message:
> I really don't think you'd find a significant difference in advantages between an upper middle class white family and an upper middle class black family.

This is a demonstrably false statement. According to Brookings:

> White children are more likely to move up the ladder while black children are more likely to fall down. 

> More than one third (37 percent) of white children born to parents in the middle income group move up to the fourth or fifth quintile, compared to only 17 percent of black children whose parents have approximately the same levels of income.

> Startlingly, almost half (45 percent) of black children whose parents were solidly middle class end up falling to the bottom of the income distribution, compared to only 16 percent of white children. Achieving middle-income status does not appear to protect black children from future economic adversity the same way it protects white children.

> Black children from poor families have poorer prospects than white children from such families. More than half (54 percent) of black children born to parents in the bottom quintile stay in the bottom, compared to 31 percent of white children.

http://www.brookings.edu/research/papers/2007/11/blackwhite-isaacs

It's not just about individual effort or cultural factors, but about systemic inequalities that affect opportunities and outcomes.

@faint-panini, I'm with you on this one. The numbers from Brookings are pretty damning. It's hard to argue with data that shows such a clear disparity in social mobility between black and white children, even when income is controlled for. @several-base, you can't just dismiss the systemic inequalities that are at play here. It's not just about individual effort or cultural factors, it's about the structural barriers that prevent black children from achieving the same level of success as their white counterparts.

message: Marx is not liberal... at all... We seem to be confusing liberal with left, and conservative with right. In reality, modern conservatism is just right leaning liberalism. Modern American liberals and conservatives both support things like capitalism and the free market.

> However, I feel like most professors try there best to promote free thought, hide their bias, and cover material in a legitimate fashion. The news stories thar come out about teachers truly over-stepping and crossing that line are outliers in the broad education system.

While I am predominantly liberal, I can definitely recognize that there is a bias, in at least the humanities, towards liberalistic thought. I majored in English and a big part of that degree is developing a theoretical background upon which you can analyze literature. And the theory we were predominantly exposed to mostly leftist, Marxist and postmodern, with philosophers like Lacan, Foucault, Kierkegaard, Butler, Althusser, Derrida, etc.

Since graduating and developing more of an interest in philosophy, I'm seeing that there are entire schools of thought I never really knew about or got to investigate because I had never been exposed to them. I was just totally in the dark when it came to so many perspectives---some of which had always been represented as a caricature to me, if at all.

So I would say the lack of representation of all viewpoints is kind of a problem, because college seems like the place where you should be exposed to a ton of different perspectives. And it makes sense that people who are already coming from a conservative perspective would be disillusioned by that lack of representation.

@baked-click, you think a few outlier news stories are the problem, but what about the entire education system being rigged against conservative views? I mean, you just admitted that your English degree was all about leftist theory, and you never even heard of other perspectives. That's not education, that's indoctrination.

@crispy-falloff, I understand your point, but let's not exaggerate - the education system is not entirely rigged against conservative views. @baked-click made a valid point that while there may be a bias in certain fields like the humanities, it's not a universal conspiracy. I've seen some great professors who make a genuine effort to present multiple perspectives. We should focus on promoting diversity of thought rather than assuming the whole system is stacked against one side.

message:
I think you're correct in some cases. Czechoslovakia split peacefully into two countries after a referendum*. People who lived in either country kept the right to live and work in the other, and now it's the EU so it hardly matters aside from state budgeting and governance. I have some caveats though: 

- What if the reason for independence is distinctly awful? The southern states in the American civil war seceded because they wanted to preserve slavery. Should we have honored their right to independence even though it was for a really terrible reason? 

- In less stable regions, this is a recipe for ethnic cleansing. The independent region might harbor resentment for the group that previously ruled them, and the chaos of a new administration is a tempting opportunity to "clean up" anybody on the wrong side of the new border. 

Edit: *Declaration of independence adopted by the Slovak national council, not a referendum. Also, there seem to be many more fans of ethnic cleansing than I anticipated.
There was no referendum about splitting Czechoslovakia. It was a political decision. Sorry for OT, had to make it clear :)

@persistent-scene, you think a peaceful split is always the way to go? What about when it's just a excuse for one group to oppress another? Like, what if the new "independent" region starts discriminating against minorities? Should we just be like "oh, they're independent now, so it's okay"? Doesn't seem right to me...

The Democrats are mad that nunes though showed that the fbi was spying on trump and his team to invent these issues and the Democrats commit the worse offense since Watergate. 

Makes me question why we continue to tolerate Democrats or the Democratic party instead of rounding them up for crimes against the fatherland

Okay lets start talking about a few of the things on your list of things that need to be addressed.

The Steele Dossier: Okay if the golden showers thing is all you took away from that dossier you literally focused on the LEAST important part taking up a few paragraphs in a 35 page doccument, and the  author states in the document was the least verified part that he had. The really important part was the the Rosneft deal. In which the dossier states that Trump was offered a 19% deal in Rosneft for running. In december [19.5%](http://washingtonmonthly.com/2017/01/11/mnuchin-needs-to-explain-the-19-5-sale-of-rosneft/) of the company was privatised, with unknown owners. On top of that there have been MANY other points within the document that have been verified.  Since it came out reporters have been able to verify a LARGE portion of the document's [listed communications](http://www.cnn.com/2017/02/10/politics/russia-dossier-update/), and its kinda suspicious that a few sources connected with the document have been [murdered](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2017/01/27/mystery-death-ex-kgb-chief-linked-mi6-spys-dossier-donald-trump/) (a bit of a putin hallmark). 

Next is the Pinging of the Russian Bank. Alfa Bank has long been implicated in Russian Money laundering schemes, but to note its not that the domain was registered with the trump organization, its that the physical server was regestered with at the headquarters of the Trump Organization. You may want to [reread the actual original implications](http://www.slate.com/articles/news_and_politics/cover_story/2016/10/was_a_server_registered_to_the_trump_organization_communicating_with_russia.html) by the computer scientists who found it. And it should be noted that Trump's real estate businesses have been tied to a recently descovered [Russian Money Laundering Scheme](http://www.newsweek.com/how-russians-launder-stolen-money-real-estate-407810). A scheme in which the whistle blower is already [dead](https://www.nytimes.com/2017/03/21/world/europe/russia-sergei-magnitsky-william-browder.html?_r=0) So yeah it may not be communication with the Trump over the elections but there are OTHER criminal possibilities and buisness ties on the table.

Next we have the problem of Trump's staff and advisors heavy connections with Russia. [Flynn](https://www.nytimes.com/2017/02/13/us/politics/donald-trump-national-security-adviser-michael-flynn.html); [Manafort](https://www.apnews.com/122ae0b5848345faa88108a03de40c5a), [Sessions](http://www.npr.org/sections/thetwo-way/2017/03/01/518058002/ag-sessions-talks-with-russian-envoy-may-be-conflict-with-senate-testimony); [Tillerson](https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2017/01/tillerson-confirmation-senate-mccain-graham-exxonmobil-russia-putin/512750/); and [many others](https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/politics/wp/2017/03/03/the-web-of-relationships-between-team-trump-and-russia/?utm_term=.f88d062a9169).

On top of that we now have [Schiff](http://www.politico.com/story/2017/03/schiff-russia-trump-collusion-236386) (A congresman and a US attorney who has prossocuted spies in the past and who has probably been one of the most level headed politicians on this whole matter); and the [FBI](http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/fbi-info-suggests-donald-trump-aides-coordinated-with-russia-a7644901.html) pointing at the evidence no longer being circumstansial in the slightest; and [McCain](http://www.msnbc.com/for-the-record-with-greta/watch/mccain-select-committee-on-russia-now-a-requirement-904215619769) now calling for a Select Committee. There has been a lot of smoke true, but denying there is any form of fire is now a bit hard to do.

message:
If Donald Trump is only saying stupid stuff because it plays well with the people who are too stupid to know he's wrong that proves my point.  

Trump won't win the election, but someone with his exact views without the pussy grabbing and temperament issues easily could have.  He pulled even in the pols before the first debate.  Also, just look at the people that we have elected.  Michelle Bachman, said CO2 can't be harmful because it is a naturally occurring compound.  

And if you think I'm cherry picking, half of the elected Republicans in Congress deny climate change.   https://thinkprogress.org/the-anti-science-climate-denier-caucus-114th-congress-edition-c76c3f8bfedd#.5rpl6q47y

I don't even want to look at how many of them deny evolution.

There's a difference between stupidity and willful ignorance + uneducation. For instance, Trump lies constantly because he can get away with it; some people don't know any better because they live in areas with poor educational systems, some people want to remain party loyal and ignore the stupid stuff he says, and other people know what he's saying is false but blank it out because the hate the other candidates more. It's not stupidity - it's a combination of factors. Added to the fire are complete lies (mainly about Clinton) but people want to believe them, so they gain traction.     
     
This election cycle is way more complicated than people want to believe. A good example are young voters, who are the mainly the ones commenting on your FB feed and Reddit posts. A part of it is not knowing what they want, i.e Bernie supporters supporting people like Trump or Johnson. A part of it is the dislike of Clinton, combined with true things that have come out, as well as lies/propaganda that are spread. The other (and the one you're probably annoyed with), is the Fuck You they are attempting to say to the political system. There's also a fair number of racist people and bigots out there, who just aren't going to vote Democrat.     
    
There's so much more to it than people being stupid. There are stupid people on both sides. There are ignorant people on both sides. But you can't just sum it up with "since these people exist, our country will remain fucked up". We deal, we carry on, and we see that in hindsight, the results will show. Trump is not going to win this election. Sure there are lots of people pissed off, but there are plenty of people who are happy the economy is recovering and we aren't sending mass amounts of troops off. Just another couple weeks.

@strong-apex, I agree with you that it's not just about stupidity, but a mix of factors. I think we also need to consider the role of misinformation and how it spreads, especially on social media. It's like a game of telephone, where false information gets repeated and amplified, and people start to believe it. And you're right, it's not just about Trump, it's about the whole system and how people are feeling disillusioned and wanting change. @nondeterministic-cabin, I think your point about elected officials denying climate change is a great example of how willful ignorance can be a problem. It's not just about individual stupidity, but about a larger cultural and political issue.

@isothermal-switch, exactly! And it's not just social media, it's also the 24-hour news cycle and the way news outlets report on things. They're more focused on getting views and ratings than actually reporting facts. And @nondeterministic-cabin, I'm not saying you're wrong about the climate change deniers, but we need to understand why people are so willing to believe that stuff. It's not just about being stupid, it's about being misinformed and misled.

@strong-apex, I completely agree with you on the role of the 24-hour news cycle and the focus on ratings over facts. It's like they're more interested in sensationalism than actual journalism. And I think that's a big part of the problem, because people are getting their information from these sources and not always fact-checking. We need to be critical of the media and demand better from them.
@isothermal-switch, @strong-apex, you guys are so extra, can we just agree that people are stupid and move on?

@persistent-scene, oh great, because oversimplifying complex issues and insulting people's intelligence is always the answer, right? How about we actually try to understand the root causes of the problem instead of just name-calling? @strong-apex and @isothermal-switch are making some valid points about the media's role in all this.

message:
I don't think you're wrong about the things you've said, but the main reason Progressives like myself are terrified is the Supreme Court. The liberal justices are older in many cases, especially RBG (hang in there!) and there is some fear that Trump may get to replace more of them. So far, his picks are abhorrent. They are clearly Pence's picks, and Pence scares me so much more than Trump, frankly. The reality is he gets to fill at least one seat. At least it's Scalia's but shit. 

There are other things that can be done with a fully Red government now that are up in the air, but for me, the big economic thing -- the road from which there is no great return is the Privatization of Medicare. I actually don't think Trump will get behind that anytime soon but he could become a big rubber stamp and he could get impeached. Both of which would lead to that possibility. The other thing I worry about, albeit mildly, is Education reform (I'm a teacher) and taking "existing funding" and giving it to vouchers which is both the Trump and GOP plan; however, a) Education reform is usually a 2nd term agenda item historically and b) it's not a main focus the moment. So I'm only slightly anxious there and realize it's unlikely to be an issue. But privatization of Medicare seems more possible. 

But, really, for me, it's the Court that could be unrecoverable, depending on how these 4 years go. 

Plus, we don't know what 4 years can bring. We really don't know. And that's terrifying. I don't think Trump gets 8 himself, but if Pence somehow is able to succeed him... ayayaye. Pence is worse for progressives than Trump! 

I am hopeful for all that you said, but I think the Supreme Court is a pretty big issue and one reason why this wasn't a win for progressives necessarily, even in the long run.

@some-hardcover I totally get where you're coming from, the Supreme Court is a huge deal and those lifetime appointments can have lasting impacts. I'm also worried about the privatization of Medicare, that would be a disaster for so many people. But I still think we can't give up hope, we have to keep fighting for what's right. And I'm with you, Pence is way scarier than Trump, at least with Trump you know what you're getting, Pence is like a wolf in sheep's clothing.

message:
That’s not the point. The point is to not have our rights (any of them) be infringed upon. The second one of our rights becomes malleable to the government, then that opens the discussion for other rights to be amended.

We already see some people who advocate “hate speech” as an offense punishable by law. That would infringe upon our first amendment right, and the more rights you give up, the more control our government has, and the easier it becomes for them to become tyrannical.

My issue with the NRA is that every time something happens they are the first to jump up and say "don't take my guns", "don't do this", "don't do that". What I never hear from them are solutions. All they care about is being able to keep their guns, and destroying any possibility of any rules that might delay them from getting more guns.

What they should be doing is working to find solutions to these problems so these "don't take my guns" situations stop arising. And their solution can't be, "give more people guns." That seems to be their solution to literally every problem. It makes them sound like idiots.

I would respect the NRA if they were at all sympathetic to the issues the country is facing and helping to find that middle ground, or other solutions that we could try, where we could start making things better. All they do is keep elected officials handcuffed and afraid to make a move on anything that can keep these kids safe.

@silver-envelope, I understand your frustration with the NRA, but let's not forget that their primary goal is to protect our second amendment rights. I agree that they should be more open to finding solutions, but we can't just dismiss their concerns about government overreach. What's to stop the government from infringing on other rights if they can easily amend the second amendment? We need to be careful not to give up our freedoms in the name of security.

@faint-panini, I see your point, but @silver-envelope has a valid concern too. The NRA does come across as pretty one-dimensional in their approach. I mean, can't they at least try to offer some alternative solutions instead of just saying "no" to every proposal? It's like they're more interested in protecting gun manufacturers' profits than actually finding common ground. And what's with all this "slippery slope" talk? It's not like we're talking about repealing the entire Bill of Rights here. Let's have a nuanced discussion for once.

I use the word karma as a general term for unpredictable consequences.

What do you mean when by "karma" when you say the "bad things" they've experienced *is karma*? I can think of a few options:

If what you mean is that the "bad things" are morally justified, then that would be a terrible argument. For one thing, the decisions of a person's ancestors thousands of years before their birth does not justify doing bad things to them today. 

But maybe what you mean is that the decisions of their ancestors are in some way causally responsible for the bad things that they've experienced. This would be empirically true, but it would be a pointless, trivial argument. For *every* person, the decisions of their ancestors are *always* causally responsible for the bad things that they experienced; for every bad thing that happens to a person, their ancestors could have made a decision such that that bad thing didn't happen. So you aren't raising some thought-provoking point that isn't immediately obvious to anyone with a minor understanding of causality. 

Or maybe you're invoking the religious notion of karma. Is that what you're doing?

So what are you actually saying here?
